voices.lens: Attorney, freelance journalist, and author who writes on gender equity, reproductive rights, and law. Brings a feminist legal lens to politics, combining her legal training with international reporting experience to analyze how policy affects women's rights.

quotes.quote: None of this is to say that the New York Times doesn’t have a general perspective. But I’m fairly confident that if you did a survey of the political leanings of every opinion piece published over the last decade, you’d see that perspective is very much on the liberal side of things.

This is not actually how it works. Op/ed editors at the Times and other publications publish all sorts of opinions they don't agree with. The idea that they only pick pieces because they support the opinion is... stunningly ignorant.

That is... just demonstrably false. 
www.nytimes.com/2023/06/13/o...

www.nytimes.com/2025/12/11/o...

www.nytimes.com/2019/08/03/o...

www.nytimes.com/interactive/...
What has the New York Times published knowing it was false? They don't always get the story right, and they issue corrections when they're wrong, but that's a pretty weighty claim to make.

They have two conservative columnists on staff, and many more liberal ones. I disagree with Douthat and Stephens, but I also honestly don't see the problem with a national newspaper including what are honestly fairly mainstream conservative voices. The "intent" is to rep a variety of views.
I mean, yes, that is the job of a newspaper op/ed page.

The last time I worked as an editor on a newspaper opinion desk was in college but yeah I published all kinds of opinions I didn't agree with! That's the job. Again, if you don't comprehend that, that's a media literacy problem on your part, and it might help to learn about how newspapers work.

Criticize the opinions! That's fair game. But suggesting that "The New York Times" as an entity agrees with the opinion of every op/ed they publish is deranged and in fact makes no sense if you think about it for more than two seconds.

We need a non partisan watchdog at the FCC, not a partisan attack dog. 

If Carr continues down this route, Democrats will hold him accountable. Threatening broadcasters’ licenses for war coverage this administration doesn’t like is the worst thing Carr has done

voices.lens: Ohio representative and House Judiciary Committee chair. Leading Republican voice on government oversight, weaponization of federal agencies, and free speech. Combative hearing style.

quotes.quote:
The Left’s template: 

Tell a lie. 

Big media reports the lie. 

Big tech amplifies the lie. 

And then if you tell the truth, they call you names. 

It’s a tale as old as time.

voices.lens: Texas-based progressive fact-checker and political commentator originally from California. Uses his platform for media literacy and has filed voting rights lawsuits. Self-described liberal.

quotes.quote: The Secretary of Fake News #news #politics #fyp

voices.lens: NBC News chief foreign correspondent who has covered every major conflict since the Iraq War. Provides ground-level reporting from war zones and authoritarian states that adds depth to foreign policy debates.

quotes.quote: A legend. We met in Baghdad in 2003 as US troops were invading.  His voice would be important today.  He’d be skeptical I suspect of where things are going now, and undoubtedly asking hard questions. .John F. Burns, Prize-winning Foreign Correspondent for The Times, Dies at 81 nytimes.com/2026/03/13

voices.lens: New Yorker staff writer and Pulitzer finalist. Korean-American who challenges mainstream narratives about Asian-American identity. Provocative essayist on race, class, and the limits of progressive politics.

quotes.quote:
I think this is well written but the gap between the media and normies on this issue is extremely wide. People just want less ads they don’t think sports betting in general is some horrific vice that will destroy young men.

voices.lens: Washington Post Tokyo bureau chief covering Japan and the Korean Peninsula. Korean-American journalist known for meticulous fact-checking and deep sourcing on Asian affairs and political accountability.

voices.lens: Pulitzer Prize-winning journalist who led the Washington Post's 'Fatal Force' project tracking police killings. CBS News correspondent. Covers race, policing, and criminal justice with deep data and sourcing.
